sound_type: nasal 'peent!' call (2000-4000 Hz) + booming dive (100-200 Hz mechanical sound from air through primaries); dual-mode: vocal + mechanical; E=5; spectacular aerial display
ecosystem: Open sky over various habitats -- urban rooftops, gravel bars, open fields, forest clearings. Most aerial nightjar. Crepuscular. Summer breeder. Migrates to South America. Urban rooftop nester (declining). 'Bull-bat' folk name.
season: Summer breeder in PNW (May-September). Migrates to South America (Amazon basin). Departs August-September. Crepuscular peak activity at twilight.
family: Caprimulgidae (2nd entry. Nightjar contrast pair with Poorwill at 145.)

coordinates.description: Common Nighthawks breed across Washington, including urban areas. Evening 'peent' calls and booming dives heard over cities and towns.

taxonomy_note.description: First Common Nighthawk entry. SECOND CAPRIMULGIDAE. Nightjar contrast pair with Poorwill (145). First genus: Chordeiles. Dual-mode sound production (vocal + mechanical). Most aerial nightjar. Urban rooftop nester. 'Bull-bat' folk name.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Chordeiles from Greek 'choreia' (dance) + 'deile' (evening) -- 'evening dancer.' minor from Latin 'smaller' (smaller than expected for its wingspan).
taxonomy_note.family_position: Second Caprimulgidae. Nightjar contrast pair: Poorwill (ground, E=3) vs. Nighthawk (aerial, E=5).

song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Two modes: peent at 2000-4000 Hz (nasal, buzzy); boom at 100-200 Hz (deep, resonant, near-subsonic).
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Peent: ~0.2 seconds each, irregular intervals. Boom: ~0.3-0.5 seconds. Display bouts: 15-30+ minutes.
song_metrics.note_structure: DUAL-MODE: Peent (single nasal note, repeated) + Boom (single mechanical pulse from dive pullout). Two sound production systems.
song_metrics.note_types: Peent (vocal, territorial/display), boom (mechanical, courtship/territorial), squeak (alarm, rare).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Display sequence: peent...peent...peent... (flying) then climb, stall, dive, BOOM. Repeat. Peenting = rhythmic base; booms = periodic punctuation.
song_metrics.timbre: Peent: nasal, buzzy, metallic. Boom: deep, resonant, almost subsonic -- felt in chest. Two radically different timbres from one bird.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=5. Peent: moderate-to-loud (300-500m). Boom: loud (500m+). Combined output high. Landscape-filling at display peak.
song_metrics.tempo: Peent: irregular, 1-3 per second during active flight. Boom: periodic, every 30-60 seconds during display. Tempo contrast: rapid peenting punctuated by infrequent booms.
song_metrics.context: Territorial display, courtship. Males perform booming dives near females and at territorial boundaries. Peak: May-July twilight. Crepuscular.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal for vocalization. Dive boom consistent across range (physics-based, not learned).
song_metrics.key_property: DUAL-MODE SOUND -- vocal 'peent!' (2000-4000 Hz) + mechanical boom (100-200 Hz, air through primaries). E=5. SECOND CAPRIMULGIDAE. Booming dive most spectacular aerial display. Nightjar contrast pair with Poorwill (145). Urban rooftop nester. Crepuscular. 'Bull-bat.' Flock migration.

plumage.overall: Dark brown-gray with intricate barring and mottling. White wing patches (visible in flight -- diagnostic). White throat band. Long, pointed wings. Forked tail.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: White wing patches in flight -- bold white bars across the primaries. In flight silhouette: long pointed wings, erratic flight, white patches flash. Peent call immediately diagnostic.
plumage.flight_pattern: Buoyant, erratic, bat-like. Long pointed wings. Sudden turns and dives pursuing insects. Display: steep dive with pullout boom.

physical_description.overall: Length: 22-25 cm. Weight: 55-98 g. Wingspan: 51-61 cm. Larger and longer-winged than Poorwill. Streamlined for sustained flight.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: White wing patches in flight. Long pointed wings. Nasal 'peent' call. Booming dive. Seen at twilight.
physical_description.field_id: Listen for nasal 'peent!' at twilight over open areas, parking lots, fields. Watch for erratic bat-like flight and white wing patches. The booming dive is unmistakable -- steep plummet followed by deep BOOM.

nodes.title: Booming Dive -- Mechanical Sound Production
nodes.description: Male dives at 60-100 km/h, air through spread primaries creates deep boom at 100-200 Hz. Felt in chest. Carries 500m+. Most spectacular aerial display in the engine. Physics: aerodynamic feather vibration.

nodes.title: Dual-Mode Sound -- Vocal + Mechanical
nodes.description: Two distinct sound production systems: 'peent' (vocal, syrinx, 2000-4000 Hz) and boom (mechanical, feathers, 100-200 Hz). Unique dual modality in the engine. Analogous to Short-eared Owl wing-clap (140).

nodes.title: Aerial Specialist -- Life on the Wing
nodes.description: Eats, drinks, and displays in flight. Continuous aerial hawking. Crepuscular activity peak. Flock migration. Most aerial nightjar. Contrast with Poorwill's ground-sitting strategy.

nodes.title: Urban Rooftop Nesting
nodes.description: Flat gravel rooftops as nesting substrate. Declining as roofs modernize (rubber membrane replaces gravel). Parents shade chicks with wings in extreme heat. Urban twilight soundscape.

nodes.title: Nightjar Contrast Pair (145-146)
nodes.description: Poorwill (E=3, ground, vocal, nocturnal, hibernates) vs. Nighthawk (E=5, aerial, dual-mode, crepuscular, migrates). Same family, opposite strategies. The two extremes of nightjar ecology.

cross_references.relationship: NIGHTJAR CONTRAST! Poorwill (E=3, ground-sitter, vocal only, nocturnal, hibernates) vs. Nighthawk (E=5, aerial, vocal+mechanical, crepuscular, migrates). Same family, opposite strategies.
MECHANICAL SOUND! Short-eared Owl wing-clap (140) and Nighthawk boom (146): both produce non-vocal mechanical sounds during flight displays. Wing-based acoustics across families.
NEXT DEGREE! Nighthawk (Caprimulgidae, E=5) to Swift (Apodidae, E=4). Nightjars to swifts. Both aerial insectivores, different families. FIRST APODIDAE.
